questions.question: What datasets did 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale' use?
questions.gold_answer: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, CIFAR-100
questions.system_answer: The paper reports pre-training on ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M. It also evaluates transfer performance on smaller benchmarks including ImageNet, ImageNet-Real, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.

questions.question: What is the parameter count of the largest variant in 'Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention'?
questions.gold_answer: The largest DeiT variant is DeiT-B★ (DeiT-B with distillation) at 87M parameters.
questions.system_answer: The largest variant listed is DeiT-B★, with 87M parameters. The paper’s reference ViT/DeiT-B model is 86M, but the starred variant is the largest in the provided evidence.

questions.question: What architectural innovations does 'Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows' introduce?
questions.gold_answer: hierarchical structure, shifted window attention
questions.system_answer: The paper introduces a hierarchical vision Transformer that computes self-attention in non-overlapping local windows, then alternates with shifted window partitioning to create cross-window connections. It also uses patch merging to build multi-scale feature maps, giving linear computational complexity with respect to image size; the evidence additionally notes the use of relative position bias.

questions.question: Briefly describe the architecture of 'Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers'.
questions.gold_answer: DINO is a self-supervised vision transformer training method
questions.system_answer: The paper presents DINO, a simple self-supervised Vision Transformer architecture based on self-distillation without labels: a student network is trained to match a momentum-teacher network across multiple augmented views, with centering and sharpening to prevent collapse. It is designed to work with both ViTs and convnets, but the paper emphasizes that ViTs with small patches benefit especially strongly.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on top-1 accuracy on ImageNet?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy in this corpus varies widely from 1.3% to 91.0% across 594 results.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows a wide spread in reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy, from 1.3 to 91.0 (stddev 8.69), so the values are not consistent. There are also multiple SOTA claims with different numbers, including CoCa (finetuned) at 91.0, PaLI-17B at 90.9, and MaxViT-XL at 88.7/88.51, which indicates conflicting top-end results across papers.

questions.question: What is the range of top-1 accuracy values reported on CIFAR-100 in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: The top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-100 in this corpus ranges from 48.5% to 91.88% across 27 results.
questions.system_answer: Across the corpus, reported CIFAR-100 top-1 accuracy values range from 48.5% to 91.88%, a spread of 43.38 percentage points. The highest reported value is 91.88% (AdaptFormer-Swin), and the evidence does not list any explicit SOTA claims for CIFAR-100.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on which model holds SOTA on ADE20K mIoU?
questions.gold_answer: No—this corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model, since 7 papers each claim SOTA on the benchmark.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU state-of-the-art model: one paper claims InternImage-H at 62.9, while another claims ViT-Adapter-L at 61.5, and SwinV2-G is also reported at 59.9. The spread of reported values is large (26.6 to 62.9), so the SOTA claim depends on which paper/result is cited.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on mIoU values on ADE20K?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across 97 results, from 26.6 to 62.9.
questions.system_answer: No. The ADE20K mIoU values in the corpus vary widely, from 26.6 to 62.9 (spread 36.3, stddev 7.29), so they are not consistent. The highest SOTA claim is InternImage-H at 62.9, while other papers report lower SOTA values such as ViT-Adapter-L at 61.5 and SwinV2-G at 59.9, indicating multiple differing SOTA claims rather than a single consistent value.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on whether position embeddings are necessary for vision transformers?
questions.gold_answer: Papers disagree on the necessity of position embeddings; some ablations show minimal impact while others demonstrate clear performance drops without them.
questions.system_answer: No, the corpus does not fully agree. Some papers treat position embeddings as part of the standard ViT design, e.g. ViT says it “adds position embeddings,” and CPVT argues that positional encodings are needed because self-attention is permutation-equivariant. But other papers report that they are not strictly necessary or have only a small effect: CvT says “Removing position embedding of CvT does not degrade performance,” and ConViT finds masking positional embeddings has a much smaller effect than in DeiT. So the evidence suggests position embeddings are often useful, but not universally necessary across vision transformers.

questions.question: How did the maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet change year over year in this corpus?
questions.system_answer: The maximum reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy increased from 85.2 in 2020 to 90.0 in 2021 and 91.0 in 2022, then declined to 87.8 in 2023 and 83.9 in 2024. So the trend was up from 2020–2022, then down over 2023–2024.

questions.question: What is the trend in average model parameter count by year in this corpus?
